children.name: Campylorhynchus
children.description: The largest wrens, boldly patterned with streaked or barred plumage and notably loud, chattering duets. Found from the southwestern United States through Central America and northern South America, often in arid scrub, cactus desert, and forest edge. Several species are highly social and cooperative breeders.

children.name: Campylorhynchus brunneicapillus
children.description: The largest North American wren and the state bird of Arizona, the Cactus Wren builds a football-shaped nest deep within chollas and other spiny cacti for protection. Its loud, repetitive churring call is one of the defining sounds of the Sonoran Desert. Pairs maintain several old nests as decoys against predators.

children.name: Campylorhynchus chiapensis
children.description: A large, boldly streaked wren restricted to the Pacific lowlands and foothills of Chiapas, Mexico. It favours tall deciduous forest with a dense understorey and is considered near-threatened due to ongoing habitat loss. Its song is a powerful, rattling series of notes delivered from prominent high perches.

children.name: Campylorhynchus fasciatus
children.description: A boldly barred wren of arid Pacific coastal scrub from Ecuador to Peru, one of the most conspicuous birds of the Tumbesian region. It forages actively through thorny thickets, often in small family groups, delivering loud rattling duets. It readily nests in and around human settlements.

children.name: Campylorhynchus griseus
children.description: A large wren of northern South America with striking white underparts contrasting with grey-brown upperparts. It inhabits open woodland, gallery forest, and scrubby savanna in Colombia, Venezuela, and Trinidad. Cooperative family groups defend territories and often breed with helpers at the nest.

children.name: Campylorhynchus gularis
children.description: A heavily spotted wren endemic to the interior highlands of Mexico, found in dry scrub, thorn forest, and rocky hillsides. Its bold spotted breast and streaked crown distinguish it from other Campylorhynchus in its range. It utters loud, emphatic churring calls that echo across arid canyons.

children.name: Campylorhynchus jocosus
children.description: Named in honour of the French naturalist Adolphe Boucard, this wren is confined to the Tehuacan Valley of Puebla and Oaxaca in southern Mexico. It inhabits dense cactus scrub dominated by organ-pipe cacti and columnar cacti, nesting within the cactus columns. Its restricted range and specialised habitat make it vulnerable to land-use change.

children.name: Campylorhynchus nuchalis
children.description: A highly social wren of the Venezuelan llanos and Colombian savannas, easily recognised by the bold black-and-white streaking on its back and crown. Groups of up to a dozen birds cooperate in raising young and defending a communal territory. Their loud, synchronised duets and choruses are among the more striking sounds of the open savannas.

children.name: Campylorhynchus rufinucha
children.description: A conspicuous, cinnamon-naped wren common in the dry Pacific lowlands from Mexico to Costa Rica. It is a cooperative breeder, living in groups that collectively build bulky, domed stick nests and share in incubation and chick-rearing duties. The species tolerates human-modified landscapes and is frequently seen around farms and villages.

children.name: Campylorhynchus turdinus
children.description: The thrush-like wren inhabits Amazonian forest undergrowth and gallery forest edges from Colombia to Brazil and Bolivia. Its heavily spotted underparts give it a thrush-like appearance unusual among Campylorhynchus. Pairs sing complex antiphonal duets that interlock precisely, sounding like a single bird.

children.name: Campylorhynchus unicolor
children.description: A plain grey-brown, unstreaked Campylorhynchus restricted to the cloud forest edges and humid montane scrub of the western Andes in Ecuador and northern Peru. It is the most range-restricted member of its genus and among the least-studied. Its subdued plumage and quiet habits make it easy to overlook.

children.name: Campylorhynchus zonatus
children.description: A boldly barred wren of humid forest and forest edge from southern Mexico to western Ecuador. It is a cooperative breeder living in noisy groups that maintain year-round territories in tall second growth and forest edge. The distinctive black-and-white barred back pattern makes it one of the most striking Campylorhynchus.

children.name: Campylorhynchus yucatanicus
children.description: An endemic wren of the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico, restricted to the coastal scrub and dry interior thorn forest of this region. It resembles the Spotted Wren but has a plainer, less heavily spotted breast and a more restricted range. Its conservation status is considered vulnerable owing to ongoing deforestation.

children.name: Campylorhynchus megalopterus
children.description: A grey-and-white barred wren endemic to the highland pine-oak forests of the Trans-Mexican Volcanic Belt and Sierra Madre del Sur. It forages in the canopy and mid-storey of mature montane forests, rarely descending to the ground. Pairs sing loud, rolling duets that are characteristic sounds of Mexican highland forests.

children.name: Campylorhynchus albobrunneus
children.description: The White-headed Wren sports a strikingly clean white head contrasting with rufous-brown upperparts, making it one of the most distinctive Campylorhynchus. It inhabits humid lowland forest in Panama and northwestern Colombia. Groups move noisily through the forest understorey and mid-levels, delivering rattling calls.

children.name: Campylorhynchus barroti
children.description: A heavily streaked, dark-capped wren of humid foothill and lowland forest in Panama and Colombia. It was long treated as a subspecies of the Band-backed Wren but is now recognised as a full species based on plumage and vocal differences. Like its congeners, it is a cooperative breeder living in extended family groups.

children.name: Salpinctes
children.description: A monotypic genus of rocky desert wrens found throughout the arid interior of western North America. Rock Wrens are highly adapted to life among boulders and cliff faces, where they construct pebble pavements leading to their nests.

children.name: Salpinctes obsoletus
children.description: The Rock Wren inhabits rocky slopes, canyon walls, and talus fields from British Columbia to Costa Rica, bobbing on its long legs and pumping its tail as it forages among boulders. It builds an elaborate nest in a rock crevice preceded by a pavement of small pebbles whose function remains debated. Its loud, ringing song carries long distances in its open, rocky habitat.

children.name: Catherpes
children.description: A monotypic genus of canyon-dwelling wrens with a remarkably long, decurved bill used to probe crevices in cliff faces and rocky outcrops in western North America.

children.name: Catherpes mexicanus
children.description: The Canyon Wren's cascading, descending song—a liquid series of whistles pouring down the scale—is one of the most evocative sounds of western North American canyons and cliff faces. It creeps spider-like over vertical rock surfaces, probing crevices with its long, slender bill for insects and spiders. Its flattened skull allows it to squeeze into surprisingly narrow rock fissures.

children.name: Hylorchilus
children.description: Two rare and poorly known wrens endemic to Mexico, associated with limestone karst outcrops and forested ravines. Both species have restricted ranges and are considered vulnerable or near-threatened.

children.name: Hylorchilus sumichrasti
children.description: Named after the Swiss-born Mexican naturalist François Sumichrast, this secretive wren is restricted to limestone karst outcrops and their associated humid forest in Veracruz and Oaxaca, Mexico. It forages among moss-covered boulders and in deep rock fissures, rarely emerging into the open. Its loud, ringing song belies the difficulty of observing this elusive bird.

children.name: Hylorchilus navai
children.description: Named after the Mexican ornithologist Jorge Nava, this recently described wren is known from a small area of limestone karst forest in Chiapas and adjacent Oaxaca and Tabasco. It is considered vulnerable owing to its very restricted range and ongoing forest clearance for agriculture. It closely resembles Sumichrast's Wren but the two species are allopatric.

children.name: Cistothorus
children.description: Marsh and grassland wrens of the Americas, typically skulking in dense vegetation and difficult to observe despite their loud, mechanical songs. Several species are marsh obligates, nesting among cattails and sedges over standing water.

children.name: Cistothorus platensis
children.description: One of the most widespread wrens in South America, the Grass Wren skulks in tall grasslands, marshy meadows, and Andean bofedales from sea level to 4,500 m. Its extraordinary morphological variation across its range has led to multiple subspecies being recognised, and some populations may represent distinct species. The mechanical, insect-like song is the most reliable means of detection.

children.name: Cistothorus apolinari
children.description: Named after the Colombian naturalist Brother Apolinar María, this critically endangered wren is confined to the reed-bed marshes of the Bogotá savanna in Colombia. Its wetland habitat has been severely reduced by drainage for agriculture and urban expansion, leaving only a few hundred birds in fragmented populations. Active habitat restoration and conservation programs are ongoing.

children.name: Cistothorus meridae
children.description: A high-altitude wren restricted to the paramo and upper montane grasslands of the Mérida Andes in Venezuela, generally above 2,500 m. It lives in dense bunch-grass and Espeletia rosette-plant formations, where it is often heard but seldom seen. Its isolated mountain range population makes it susceptible to climate-driven upslope habitat contraction.

children.name: Cistothorus stellaris
children.description: The Sedge Wren is a secretive inhabitant of wet meadows, sedge marshes, and moist grasslands across North America, wintering south to Central America. Males build numerous dummy nests in addition to the real nest, a behaviour thought to confuse nest predators. Its dry, staccato chipping and buzzing song is often the only indication of its presence in dense sedge cover.

children.name: Cistothorus palustris
children.description: The Marsh Wren is a quintessential North American cattail marsh bird, building multiple dummy nests throughout its territory in an aggressive display of fitness. Males may mate polygynously and destroy eggs in neighbouring males' nests, an unusual example of inter-male nest sabotage. Its loud, bubbling, mechanical song fills freshwater marshes from coast to coast.

children.name: Thryomanes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Bewick's Wren, a widespread and adaptable wren of western North America known for its exceptionally varied and musical song.

children.name: Thryomanes bewickii
children.description: Named by John James Audubon in honour of his friend, the engraver Thomas Bewick, this wren is famous for the extraordinary variety of its song—males learn a local 'dialect' of up to 22 distinct song types from neighbours. Once common across eastern North America, it has undergone a dramatic decline east of the Mississippi, possibly due to competition with the House Wren. It remains abundant in shrubby western habitats from British Columbia to Mexico.

children.name: Thryophilus
children.description: Middle American and northern South American wrens of forest edge and thickets, characterised by warm rufous or brown tones and often bold face patterns. Several species are restricted to narrow geographic ranges.

children.name: Thryophilus sinaloa
children.description: A rufous-backed wren of the Pacific slope deciduous and semi-deciduous forests of northwestern Mexico, from Sinaloa south to Jalisco. It inhabits dense riparian thickets and thorn forest, where its rich, fluty song is a characteristic sound of the dry season. Formerly placed in Thryothorus, molecular studies prompted its reclassification.

children.name: Thryophilus pleurostictus
children.description: The Banded Wren is a boldly barred-flanked wren of the dry Pacific lowlands from Mexico to Costa Rica, inhabiting dense thorn scrub and deciduous forest understorey. Its rich, varied song is among the finest of the Central American wrens. It is a characteristic bird of the Mesoamerican dry forest biodiversity hotspot.

children.name: Thryophilus rufalbus
children.description: A strikingly bicoloured wren with deep rufous upperparts and gleaming white underparts, found in dry and semi-humid forest from Honduras to western Ecuador. It is an accomplished songster, and pairs perform elaborate, melodious duets in the shaded forest understorey. Despite its striking appearance, it can be surprisingly difficult to see in dense vegetation.

children.name: Thryophilus spadix
children.description: A dark-capped, rufous-brown wren of humid lowland and foothill forest in Panama and northwestern Colombia, associated with dense undergrowth near streams and forest edges. It was long considered a subspecies of the Rufous-and-white Wren but differs in its dark sooty-grey head and more restricted range. Its loud duets are characteristic sounds of wet lowland forest.

children.name: Thryophilus nicefori
children.description: Named after the Colombian ornithologist Brother Nicéforo María, this critically endangered wren is known from a tiny area in the Chicamocha Canyon of Colombia, where it inhabits dry scrub on canyon walls and slopes. Fewer than 250 mature individuals are estimated to exist, making it one of the most threatened birds in the Americas. Ongoing deforestation and overgrazing continue to degrade its already tiny range.

children.name: Pheugopedius
children.description: A genus of mostly Middle American and northern South American wrens with boldly patterned faces and rich, complex songs. Many species were formerly placed in Thryothorus; molecular analysis prompted a widespread reclassification.

children.name: Pheugopedius euophrys
children.description: A richly coloured wren of Andean cloud forest undergrowth in Ecuador and Peru, notable for its unbarred tail that distinguishes it from most congeners. It inhabits dense bamboo thickets and forest borders at mid-elevations, where pairs duet loudly despite rarely emerging into the open. Its elaborate vocal repertoire suggests a high degree of pair-bond coordination.

children.name: Pheugopedius eisenmanni
children.description: Named after the ornithologist Eugene Eisenmann, the Inca Wren is a strikingly marked species restricted to the steep, humid scrub-covered slopes of the Urubamba Canyon near Machu Picchu in Peru. It is a Peruvian endemic found only at mid-elevations in dense riverside vegetation and polylepis-forest edges. Its restricted range and specialised habitat place it among Peru's most notable avian endemics.

children.name: Pheugopedius atrogularis
children.description: The Black-throated Wren is a boldly marked species with a striking black throat patch, found in humid lowland and foothill forests from Nicaragua to Ecuador. It skulks in dense undergrowth near streams and forest edges, delivering powerful, ringing songs. It is closely related to the Sooty-headed Wren and may form a superspecies with it.

children.name: Pheugopedius genibarbis
children.description: The Moustached Wren is widely distributed in the undergrowth of humid Amazonian and Atlantic Forest across tropical South America. It sports a prominent white supercilium and black malar stripe giving it a moustached appearance. Its elaborate, melodious duets ring through the dense forest understorey.

children.name: Pheugopedius felix
children.description: True to its name, the Happy Wren delivers one of the most exuberant and musical songs in the genus, a rich, bubbling series of whistles that carries far through the dry thorn forests of the Mexican Pacific slope. It inhabits dense undergrowth in semi-arid scrub and deciduous forest from Sinaloa to Oaxaca. Males and females sing in precise duet coordination.

children.name: Pheugopedius maculipectus
children.description: A boldly spotted-breasted wren of the humid Atlantic and Pacific lowlands from Mexico to Costa Rica, inhabiting thick undergrowth at forest borders, second growth, and overgrown clearings. It is an accomplished songster, often performing duets with its mate in the dense vegetation. Its spotted breast is unique among Central American Pheugopedius.

children.name: Pheugopedius mystacalis
children.description: The Whiskered Wren is a richly patterned species of the Andean foothills and sub-Andean forest in Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. Its bold black-and-white facial pattern with prominent 'whiskers' makes it one of the more distinctive Pheugopedius. Pairs maintain territories in dense, humid undergrowth and engage in complex antiphonal duets.

children.name: Pheugopedius coraya
children.description: The Coraya Wren is a widely distributed Amazonian species with a bold black throat and breast patch, found from Venezuela and Colombia south to Bolivia and Brazil. It inhabits dense undergrowth at the edges of terra firme forest and along stream margins. Males and females coordinate their songs in elaborate antiphonal duets.

children.name: Pheugopedius rutilus
children.description: A richly rufous-breasted wren of humid forest undergrowth from Costa Rica to Colombia and Venezuela, characterised by its warm orange-rufous breast contrasting with a streaked black-and-white head. It inhabits dense thickets and tangled vegetation at forest borders and in second growth. Its bright colouration and loud song make it one of the more conspicuous Pheugopedius.

children.name: Pheugopedius schlegelii
children.description: Named after the Dutch ornithologist Hermann Schlegel, this Andean foothills wren ranges from Colombia to Bolivia in humid subtropical and tropical forest undergrowth. Its speckled breast and streaked head distinguish it from similar congeners. It is most often encountered in dense tangled vegetation along forest borders and ravines.

children.name: Pheugopedius sclateri
children.description: Named after the British ornithologist Philip Sclater, the Spiny-faced Wren is a boldly spotted species of western Amazonian lowlands in Ecuador and Peru. The name refers to its heavily streaked and spotted facial pattern rather than any literal spines. It inhabits undergrowth of humid terra firme and várzea forest, often near streams.

children.name: Pheugopedius solstitialis
children.description: A richly streaked wren of Andean cloud forest in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru, often encountered in bamboo-dominated undergrowth and dense forest borders at 1,500–2,800 m. Its strongly streaked throat distinguishes it from most congeners. Pairs produce interlocking antiphonal duets that ring through the cloud forest.

children.name: Thryothorus
children.description: The largest wren genus in terms of species richness outside of Campylorhynchus reclassification, centred on the Americas. Carolina Wrens and their allies are noted for their powerful, musical songs and strong pair bonds maintained throughout the year.

children.name: Thryothorus ludovicianus
children.description: One of the most familiar and beloved garden birds of eastern North America, the Carolina Wren delivers a loud, ringing 'teakettle-teakettle-teakettle' song year-round, including on the coldest winter days. Pairs are monogamous and maintain permanent territories, roosting together in nest boxes and dense vegetation. Its rich rufous plumage and bold white supercilium make it one of the most handsome of the North American wrens.

children.name: Thryothorus longirostris
children.description: The Long-billed Wren is a richly coloured caatinga endemic of northeastern Brazil, inhabiting dry thorn scrub and gallery forest in one of the world's largest tropical dry forest ecosystems. Its proportionally long bill is used to probe deeply into bark and leaf litter. It delivers a rich, varied song typical of the genus.

children.name: Thryothorus thoracicus
children.description: A small, boldly streaked wren of the Caribbean slope rainforests of Costa Rica and Panama, preferring dense undergrowth in the vicinity of water. It is most reliably located by its distinctive song, a rapid series of clear whistles. The species has a disjunct distribution, reflecting specialised habitat preferences.

children.name: Thryothorus nigricapillus
children.description: The Bay Wren is a strikingly patterned species with a black cap, white face, and rich rufous body, inhabiting mangroves, swamp forest, and humid lowland thicket from Honduras to Ecuador. It is equally at home in dense mangrove fringe as in humid forest undergrowth, and is never found far from water. Pairs produce powerful, rolling duets that carry well through the dense coastal vegetation.

children.name: Thryothorus modestus
children.description: The Plain Wren is a relatively unmarked, modestly coloured wren of the dry Pacific lowlands and foothills from Mexico to Costa Rica. It inhabits dense, low scrub, overgrown clearings, and forest borders, where it delivers a rich, melodious song incongruous with its subdued appearance. Three subspecies show subtle differences in plumage tone across the range.

children.name: Thryothorus leucotis
children.description: A widespread and adaptable wren of South American lowlands, ranging from Panama to Bolivia and Brazil, found in second growth, forest borders, and riparian thickets. Its warm buff underparts and white-streaked face distinguish it from similar species. It delivers a rich, bubbling song and readily adapts to human-modified landscapes.

children.name: Thryothorus superciliaris
children.description: A boldly superciliated wren of the Tumbesian dry forests and scrub of Ecuador and Peru, one of the characteristic birds of this threatened biodiversity hotspot. It inhabits dense thorn scrub and deciduous forest borders, where it creeps through low vegetation with great agility. Its song is a rich, varied series of whistles and trills.

children.name: Thryothorus galbraithii
children.description: Named after the British ornithologist John Galbraith, this Colombian endemic inhabits dense undergrowth in humid lowland forest on the Pacific slope of the western Andes. It is poorly known and rarely observed, with few modern records confirming its occurrence outside of the type locality. Its conservation status is considered near-threatened owing to ongoing deforestation.

children.name: Thryothorus semibadius
children.description: The Riverside Wren is a boldly patterned species restricted to dense undergrowth along streams and rivers in southern Central America. It has a very restricted range in Costa Rica, Panama, and extreme northwestern Colombia, making it one of the more geographically limited Thryothorus. Its association with forested riparian corridors means it is particularly vulnerable to river bank clearance.

children.name: Thryothorus fasciatoventris
children.description: The Black-bellied Wren is a striking species with a boldly barred black-and-white belly, found in humid lowland and foothill forest in southern Central America and northwestern South America. It is most often encountered in dense undergrowth near streams and forest borders. Its powerful, ringing song is among the most melodious of the Central American wrens.

children.name: Thryothorus guarayanus
children.description: The Fawn-breasted Wren inhabits the Chiquitano dry forest and cerrado woodland of eastern Bolivia and western Brazil, a region of exceptional biological diversity. Its plain fawn underparts and relatively subdued plumage distinguish it from its more boldly patterned congeners. It is considered one of the characteristic birds of the Bolivian transition zone between Amazonia and the Chaco.

children.name: Thryothorus leucopogon
children.description: The Stripe-breasted Wren is found in humid lowland and foothill forest undergrowth in Colombia and Ecuador. Its strongly streaked breast and flanks make it one of the more distinctively patterned members of the genus. It inhabits dense thickets and tangled vegetation at forest borders and along forested waterways.

children.name: Thryothorus atricapilloides
children.description: The Pale-breasted Wren inhabits the arid coastal scrub, dry interior valleys, and thorn woodland of Peru, Ecuador, and extreme northern Chile. Its paler, buff-white underparts and less distinct facial markings distinguish it from the Buff-breasted Wren, which replaces it in more humid habitats to the north. It is among the most drought-tolerant wrens and can be found in some of the driest coastal desert scrub in South America.

children.name: Troglodytes
children.description: For their size, wrens produce one of the loudest songs of any bird — a rapid powerful torrent of trills carrying hundreds of metres through dense cover. In harsh Scandinavian winters dozens of wrens will huddle together in a nest box for warmth. One of Sweden's most widespread and numerous species despite its tiny size.

children.name: Troglodytes troglodytes
children.description: A tiny, round, cock-tailed bird that delivers an astonishingly loud, sustained trilling song from deep cover. In hard Scandinavian winters wrens huddle communally in nest boxes and cavities for warmth.

children.name: Troglodytes aedon
children.description: The House Wren is the most widespread wren in the Americas, breeding from Canada to Tierra del Fuego and readily adapting to nest boxes, gardens, and suburban areas. Males famously destroy eggs and nestlings in nest boxes used by other species to commandeer cavities for their own polygynous mating system. Its bubbly, exuberant song is one of the most familiar sounds of gardens throughout the Western Hemisphere.

children.name: Troglodytes musculus
children.description: Long treated as a subspecies of the House Wren, the Southern House Wren is now recognised as a distinct species distributed across South America east of the Andes. It is one of the most numerous and adaptable songbirds in South America, equally at home in urban parks and remote forest edge. It differs from the House Wren in subtle plumage tones and vocal nuances.

children.name: Troglodytes solstitialis
children.description: A small, energetic wren of Andean cloud forests from Venezuela to Bolivia, foraging actively through mossy branches, epiphytes, and dense undergrowth at 2,000–3,500 m. It resembles a miniature version of the Eurasian Wren in both form and behaviour, and like that species delivers a disproportionately loud and powerful song. The species name refers to its discovery near the solstice.

children.name: Troglodytes monticola
children.description: An endemic wren confined entirely to the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta in Colombia, one of the world's most important centres of bird endemism. It inhabits elfin forest, paramo, and the uppermost cloud forest above 2,500 m, and is rarely encountered below the treeline. Its highly restricted range and potential vulnerability to climate-driven habitat shifts make it a conservation priority.

children.name: Troglodytes rufulus
children.description: The Tepui Wren is a richly rufous-tinged species endemic to the ancient sandstone table-top mountains (tepuis) of the Guiana Highlands in Venezuela, Guyana, and adjacent Brazil. It inhabits the cloud forest and shrubby vegetation of tepui summits, often above 1,500 m. Like many tepui endemics, it is poorly known due to the remoteness and difficulty of access of its habitat.

children.name: Troglodytes sissonii
children.description: Now recognised as a distinct species, this wren is restricted to the coastlines and offshore islands of the Falkland Islands (Malvinas), where it forages along rocky shores, kelp beds, and stream margins. It is one of the few passerine birds endemic to the Falklands and is considered near-threatened due to predation by introduced mammals on some islands. Note: IOC v14 uses sissonii for the Falkland Islands Wren.

children.name: Troglodytes tanneri
children.description: The Clarion Wren is endemic to Clarión Island in the Revillagigedo Archipelago off the Pacific coast of Mexico, one of the most isolated island groups in the eastern Pacific. Named after the collector who obtained the type specimen, it inhabits dense vegetation on this remote volcanic island. Its restricted island range makes it vulnerable to introduced predators and human disturbance.

children.name: Troglodytes cobbi
children.description: Named after Arthur Frederick Cobbe, an early naturalist who studied the wildlife of the Falkland Islands, this wren is restricted to rat-free offshore islands in the Falkland archipelago. It forages fearlessly along rocky coastlines and kelp-strewn shores, probing crevices for invertebrates. Eradication of introduced rats from key islands has allowed populations to recover significantly.

children.name: Uropsila
children.description: A monotypic genus of small, round wrens of the forest understorey of Mexico and Central America, related to Henicorhina and sharing its skull-like creeping behaviour through dense tangles.

children.name: Uropsila leucogastra
children.description: A tiny, almost tailless wren of the forest understorey in the Yucatan Peninsula and adjacent regions of Mexico, Belize, and Guatemala. It creeps through dense low vegetation and tangled roots in the manner of a Wood Wren, gleaning small insects from leaves and bark. Despite its small range it is locally common in suitable humid and semi-humid forest.

children.name: Henicorhina
children.description: The Wood-Wrens are small, almost tailless wrens of the Neotropical montane forest understorey, renowned for their extraordinarily powerful and melodious songs. Several recently described species occupy very narrow altitudinal and geographic ranges in the Andes.

children.name: Henicorhina leucosticta
children.description: The White-breasted Wood-Wren is a common but secretive bird of humid lowland and foothill forest from southern Mexico to Bolivia, creeping mouse-like through dense undergrowth. Despite its small size it produces a powerful, ringing song that carries far through the forest. It forages alone or in pairs, rarely joining mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Henicorhina leucophrys
children.description: The Gray-breasted Wood-Wren is a montane counterpart to the White-breasted, replacing it at higher elevations in Andean cloud forests from Mexico to Bolivia. It inhabits dense bamboo thickets, mossy undergrowth, and tangled fallen vegetation at 1,200–3,000 m. Its complex song is highly variable across its extensive range, and the species complex likely contains several cryptic species.

children.name: Henicorhina leucoptera
children.description: Described as recently as 2010, the Bar-winged Wood-Wren is a narrowly endemic species of the east Andean cloud forests in northern Peru. It is distinguished by prominent white wing-bars absent in its congeners. Its very limited range and dependence on pristine cloud forest make it among the most recently discovered and conservation-sensitive members of the genus.

children.name: Henicorhina negreti
children.description: Described in 2003, the Munchique Wood-Wren is a critically endangered species known only from a tiny area of cloud forest on Cerro Munchique in the western Andes of Colombia. Named after the Colombian conservationist Álvaro José Negret, fewer than 250 individuals may survive in its cloud-shrouded mountain habitat. It represents one of the most striking examples of Andean microendemism.

children.name: Henicorhina anachoreta
children.description: Described in 2003 from the Santa Marta Mountains in Colombia, the Hermit Wood-Wren is a tiny endemic of the upper cloud forest in this extraordinary centre of endemism. The species name means 'hermit' or 'recluse', reflecting its secretive habits and restricted, isolated range. It is closely related to the Gray-breasted Wood-Wren but differs in vocalisation and subtle plumage details.

children.name: Henicorhina darienensis
children.description: The Darien Wood-Wren was described in 2012 and is restricted to the cloud forests of the Darien region on the Colombia-Panama border. It had long been overlooked due to its superficial similarity to the Gray-breasted Wood-Wren but is now recognised as a distinct species based on song differences and genetic evidence. The Darien region's inaccessibility has contributed to the late discovery of this species.

children.name: Henicorhina bangsi
children.description: Named in honour of the American ornithologist Outram Bangs, this wood-wren is a Colombian endemic associated with the cloud forests of the Western and Central Andes. It has a very restricted distribution and is considered near-threatened due to extensive deforestation within its range. Recent molecular work supports its recognition as a species distinct from the Gray-breasted Wood-Wren.

children.name: Microcerculus
children.description: The nightingale-wrens are tiny, almost tailless wrens of the humid Neotropical forest floor, noted for their extraordinarily long, pure-toned songs. They creep through leaf litter and among roots on the forest floor in the manner of mice.

children.name: Microcerculus marginatus
children.description: The Scaly-breasted Wren is a secretive denizen of the Neotropical rainforest floor from Panama to Brazil, foraging alone through leaf litter and among fallen logs for small insects. Its extraordinary song—a series of long, pure whistles rising in pitch over several minutes—can be heard far through the forest but the singer is almost impossible to see. The scaly scaling on the breast gives this tiny wren an almost reptilian appearance.

children.name: Microcerculus bambla
children.description: The Wing-banded Wren is an Amazonian floor-dweller distinguished by bold white wing-bars that are unique among Microcerculus. It inhabits the floor and understorey of undisturbed humid terra firme forest in Venezuela, the Guianas, and northern Brazil. Like its congeners, it produces a hauntingly pure, ascending whistled song audible from considerable distances.

children.name: Microcerculus ustulatus
children.description: The Flutist Wren inhabits the forest floor of Andean foothills and lower cloud forests from Venezuela to Peru, noted for its extraordinarily musical, flute-like song. It forages alone among leaf litter and moss-covered roots, ascending only rarely into the understorey. Its name is apt—its sustained, complex whistled phrases resemble a skilled human flautist.

children.name: Microcerculus philomela
children.description: Named after Philomela, the nightingale of Greek mythology, the Nightingale Wren is considered to have the most complex and beautiful song of any Central American bird, comprising hundreds of distinct phrases. It inhabits the floor of humid lowland and foothill forest from Mexico to Panama, almost never leaving dense cover. Discovering this virtuoso performer in the forest requires great patience—it is far more often heard than seen.

children.name: Cinnycerthia
children.description: The fulvous wrens are a group of large, richly coloured wrens of Andean cloud forests and high-elevation scrub. Closely related to the wood-wrens, they differ in their larger size and typically plain or lightly streaked rufous-brown plumage.

children.name: Cinnycerthia unirufa
children.description: A large, entirely rufous wren of the Andean cloud forest understory from Colombia to Bolivia, often foraging in noisy mixed-species flocks with tanagers and other insectivores. Its strikingly uniform, rich rufous plumage and relatively large size make it one of the most distinctive Andean wrens. It frequents bamboo thickets and dense undergrowth at elevations between 2,000 and 3,500 m.

children.name: Cinnycerthia peruana
children.description: The Peruvian Wren is a large, cinnamon-brown wren of Andean cloud forest in Peru and adjacent Bolivia, closely related to the Rufous Wren. It inhabits dense undergrowth and bamboo thickets at 2,000–3,000 m, and like its congeners is more often heard than seen. Its taxonomic status relative to other Cinnycerthia has been the subject of recent debate.

children.name: Cinnycerthia olivascens
children.description: Named after the British ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe, this wren is found in the cloud forests of the northern Andes in Colombia and northern Ecuador. It is somewhat plainer and more olivaceous than the Rufous Wren and tends to occupy slightly lower elevations. Sharpe's Wren inhabits dense, mossy undergrowth and tangled bamboo in mature cloud forest.

children.name: Cinnycerthia fulva
children.description: The Fulvous Wren is restricted to the eastern Andes of central Colombia and adjacent Ecuador, inhabiting cloud forest and forest borders at 1,800–2,800 m. Its warm fulvous-brown plumage is less richly rufous than the Rufous Wren. It is closely associated with bamboo stands within the cloud forest and may depend on Chusquea bamboo for nesting.

children.name: Cinnycerthia cerverai
children.description: Named after the Spanish ornithologist Francisco Javier Cervera, the Sepia-brown Wren is the most recently described member of the genus, known from the cloud forests of northern Peru. It is distinguished from other Cinnycerthia by its darker, more uniformly sepia-brown plumage with less rufous tones. Its restricted range and specialised cloud forest habitat place it among Peru's most threatened passerines.

children.name: Odontorchilus
children.description: Two unusual wrens of the Neotropical forest canopy and sub-canopy, unique in the family for their predominantly arboreal, treetop-foraging habits. Both species are rarely encountered at lower levels.

children.name: Odontorchilus cinereus
children.description: The Tooth-billed Wren is a small Amazonian wren named for the minute serrations on the tomia of its bill, a feature unique among Troglodytidae. It forages in the canopy and sub-canopy of humid terra firme forest in the western Amazon basin, a habitat unusual for the wren family. Despite its arboreal habits it retains the characteristic wren body plan of a cocked tail and round body.

children.name: Odontorchilus branickii
children.description: Named after the Polish naturalist Konstanty Branicki, the Gray-mantled Wren is a canopy-foraging wren of Andean foothill and cloud forest from Colombia to Bolivia. Its grey mantle and wings contrast with its rufous crown and underparts. It forages among epiphytes and mossy branches in the forest canopy, well above the forest floor typical of most other wrens.

children.name: Cyphorhinus
children.description: The song-wrens of the Neotropical forest floor and understorey, celebrated for producing some of the most hauntingly beautiful songs in the bird world. Their complex, organlike music has been compared to human choral singing.

children.name: Cyphorhinus aradus
children.description: The Musician Wren is celebrated throughout the Amazon basin for its extraordinary, organlike song—a series of pure, mellow notes of different pitches that sound like a chord being played on an instrument. It inhabits the floor and understorey of undisturbed humid forest and is rarely seen above eye level. Amazonian indigenous peoples have long revered its song, and it is considered one of the finest singers among all birds.

children.name: Cyphorhinus modulator
children.description: The Varied Song-Wren is found in humid lowland and foothill forest in northeastern South America, including the Guiana Shield region and northern Brazil. Its song is among the most variable of any Cyphorhinus, with different populations producing distinct song types. It inhabits the forest floor and lower understorey of undisturbed terra firme and seasonally flooded forest.

children.name: Cyphorhinus phaeocephalus
children.description: The Song Wren inhabits the floor and understorey of humid lowland and foothill forest from Honduras to Ecuador, and like its Amazonian relatives produces a hauntingly beautiful, melodious song. It is a shy skulker of the dense forest floor, rarely emerging into the open. The Song Wren is often the only Cyphorhinus present in Central American forests and represents the family at its musical zenith in that region.

children.name: Cyphorhinus thoracicus
children.description: A richly coloured song-wren with a warm chestnut breast, inhabiting the Andean foothills and lower cloud forest from Colombia to Bolivia. It forages on the forest floor among leaf litter and mossy roots, producing its extraordinary, choral-sounding song from the dim understorey. Its elevated altitudinal range (800–2,000 m) distinguishes it from the lowland Musician Wren.

children.name: Donacobius
children.description: A monotypic genus of a strikingly patterned marsh bird of South America, sometimes placed in its own family Donacobiidae. The Black-capped Donacobius is unique in combining a wren-like body plan with social complexity and vocal duetting reminiscent of Old World babblers.

children.name: Donacobius atricapilla
children.description: The Black-capped Donacobius is a boldly patterned, socially complex bird of South American marshes, reed beds, and lake margins from Panama to Argentina. Pairs perform exuberant, synchronised duets while wagging their long tails and inflating bright orange gular pouches, producing one of the most dramatic vocal displays in the Neotropics. Its true phylogenetic affinities have long been debated, and it is now sometimes placed in its own family, Donacobiidae.
